sources.title: Pearson Edexcel International GCSE Further Pure Mathematics specification

statements.statementText: Use algebra and calculus to set up and solve problems.
statements.sourceLocator: Official specification PDF page 15, line IDs p15-r4-b0-l0, Edexcel-4PM1-OVERVIEW-ALGC

reviewNotes: Completing the square is a method for solving quadratic problems.
Solving linear inequalities is an algebraic problem-solving skill.
Using graph intersections to solve equations.
Derivative as rate of change is a calculus tool for solving problems.

statements.statementText: Develop competence and confidence when manipulating mathematical expressions.
statements.sourceLocator: Official specification PDF page 15, line IDs p15-r6-b0-l0, Edexcel-4PM1-OVERVIEW-ALGM

reviewNotes: Collecting like terms is a fundamental manipulation.
Expanding products is a key manipulation.
Factorisation is a core algebraic manipulation.
Completing the square is an important algebraic manipulation technique.

statements.statementText: Use coordinate geometry.
statements.sourceLocator: Official specification PDF page 15, line IDs p15-r14-b0-l0, Edexcel-4PM1-OVERVIEW-COOR

reviewNotes: Core concept of coordinate geometry.
Gradient is a fundamental part of coordinate geometry.
Circle equation is a key topic in coordinate geometry.

statements.statementText: Understand the properties of shapes, angles and transformations.
statements.sourceLocator: Official specification PDF page 15, line IDs p15-r10-b0-l0, Edexcel-4PM1-OVERVIEW-GEOM

statements.statementText: Construct and use graphs in a range of situations.
statements.sourceLocator: Official specification PDF page 15, line IDs p15-r8-b0-l0, Edexcel-4PM1-OVERVIEW-GRPH

reviewNotes: Linear graphs are a basic graph type.
Quadratic graphs are a common graph type.
Vertical translation is a way to construct transformed graphs.
Using graph intersections is a key application.

statements.statementText: Use numerical skills in a purely mathematical way and in real-life situations.
statements.sourceLocator: Official specification PDF page 15, line IDs p15-r2-b0-l0, Edexcel-4PM1-OVERVIEW-NUM

reviewNotes: Inverse operations are a fundamental numerical skill.
Estimation is commonly used in real-life numerical applications.
Problem-solving cycle can involve numerical skills in real-world contexts.
Real-life applications often require abstraction and simplification.

statements.statementText: Use trigonometry.
statements.sourceLocator: Official specification PDF page 15, line IDs p15-r16-b0-l0, Edexcel-4PM1-OVERVIEW-TRIG

statements.statementText: Use vectors and rates of change to model situations.
statements.sourceLocator: Official specification PDF page 15, line IDs p15-r12-b0-l0, Edexcel-4PM1-OVERVIEW-VECT

statements.statementText: Students will be expected to have a thorough knowledge of the content common to the Pearson Edexcel International GCSE in Mathematics (Specification A) (Higher Tier) or Pearson Edexcel International GCSE in Mathematics (Specification B).
statements.sourceLocator: Official specification PDF page 15, line IDs p15-r17-b0-l0,p15-r17-b0-l1,p15-r17-b0-l2, p15-r17-b0-l0

statements.statementText: Questions may be set which assumes knowledge of some topics covered in these specifications, however knowledge of statistics and matrices will not be required.
statements.sourceLocator: Official specification PDF page 15, line IDs p15-r17-b1-l0,p15-r17-b1-l1, p15-r17-b1-l0

statements.statementText: Students will be expected to carry out arithmetic and algebraic manipulation, such as being able to change the subject of a formula and evaluate numerically the value of any variable in a formula, given the values of the other variables.
statements.sourceLocator: Official specification PDF page 15, line IDs p15-r17-b2-l0,p15-r17-b2-l1,p15-r17-b2-l2, p15-r17-b2-l0

conceptLinks.evidenceSpan: change the subject of a formula
reviewNotes: Directly mentioned skill.

conceptLinks.evidenceSpan: evaluate numerically the value of any variable in a formula, given the values of the other variables
reviewNotes: Directly mentioned skill of substitution.

statements.statementText: The use and notation of set theory will be adopted where appropriate.
statements.sourceLocator: Official specification PDF page 15, line IDs p15-r17-b3-l0, p15-r17-b3-l0

statements.statementText: Each paper is assessed through a 2-hour examination set and marked by Pearson.
statements.sourceLocator: Official specification PDF page 16, line IDs p16-r0-b1-l0, p16-r0-b1-l0

statements.statementText: The total number of marks for each paper is 100.
statements.sourceLocator: Official specification PDF page 16, line IDs p16-r0-b2-l0, p16-r0-b2-l0

statements.statementText: Each paper will consist of around 11 questions with varying mark allocations per question, which will be stated on the paper.
statements.sourceLocator: Official specification PDF page 16, line IDs p16-r0-b3-l0,p16-r0-b3-l1, p16-r0-b3-l0

statements.statementText: Each paper will contain questions from any part of the specification content, and the solution of any questions may require knowledge of more than one section of the specification content.
statements.sourceLocator: Official specification PDF page 16, line IDs p16-r0-b4-l0,p16-r0-b4-l1,p16-r0-b4-l2, p16-r0-b4-l0

statements.statementText: Each paper will have approximately 40% of the marks distributed evenly over grades 4 and 5 and approximately 60% of the marks distributed evenly over grades 6, 7, 8 and 9.
statements.sourceLocator: Official specification PDF page 16, line IDs p16-r0-b5-l0,p16-r0-b5-l1, p16-r0-b5-l0

statements.statementText: Diagrams will not necessarily be drawn to scale and measurements should not be taken from diagrams unless instructions to this effect are given.
statements.sourceLocator: Official specification PDF page 16, line IDs p16-r0-b6-l0,p16-r0-b6-l1, p16-r0-b6-l0

statements.statementText: A formulae sheet (Appendix 4) will be included in the written examinations.
statements.sourceLocator: Official specification PDF page 16, line IDs p16-r0-b7-l0, p16-r0-b7-l0

statements.statementText: A calculator may be used in the examinations (please see page 22 for further information).
statements.sourceLocator: Official specification PDF page 16, line IDs p16-r0-b8-l0, p16-r0-b8-l0

statements.statementText: Questions will be set in SI units (international system of units).
statements.sourceLocator: Official specification PDF page 16, line IDs p16-r0-b9-l0, p16-r0-b9-l0

statements.statementText: The functions a^x and log_b x (where b is a natural number greater than one)
notesText: A knowledge of the shape of the graphs of a^x and log_b x is expected, but not a formal expression for the gradient.
statements.sourceLocator: Official specification PDF page 17, line IDs p17-r2-b1-l0,p17-r5-b0-l0,p17-r5-b0-l1,p17-r6-b0-l0,p17-r6-b0-l1,p17-r6-b0-l2,p17-r6-b0-l3, 1.A

conceptLinks.evidenceSpan: The functions a^x and log_b x (where b is a natural number greater than one)
reviewNotes: Directly names the exponential function with base a.

conceptLinks.evidenceSpan: The functions a^x and log_b x (where b is a natural number greater than one)
reviewNotes: Directly names the logarithmic function as the inverse of the exponential.

statements.statementText: Use and properties of indices and logarithms, including change of base
notesText: To include: logₐ(xy) = logₐ x + logₐ y; logₐ(x/y) = logₐ x − logₐ y; logₐ(xᵏ) = k logₐ x; logₐ a = 1; logₐ 1 = 0. The solution of equations of the form aˣ = b. Students may use the change of base formulae logₐ x = logᵦ x / logᵦ a and logₐ b = 1 / logᵦ a.

conceptLinks.evidenceSpan: Use and properties of indices and logarithms, including change of base
reviewNotes: Codex replaced taxonomy-container links with atomic leaves (knowledge-v5-leaf-closure-round27-1).

conceptLinks.evidenceSpan: Use and properties of indices and logarithms, including change of base
reviewNotes: Codex replaced taxonomy-container links with atomic leaves (knowledge-v5-leaf-closure-round27-1).

conceptLinks.evidenceSpan: Use and properties of indices and logarithms, including change of base
reviewNotes: Codex replaced taxonomy-container links with atomic leaves (knowledge-v5-leaf-closure-round27-1).

conceptLinks.evidenceSpan: Use and properties of indices and logarithms, including change of base
reviewNotes: Codex replaced taxonomy-container links with atomic leaves (knowledge-v5-leaf-closure-round27-1).

reviewNotes: Codex replaced taxonomy-container links with atomic leaves (knowledge-v5-leaf-closure-round27-1).

reviewNotes: Codex replaced taxonomy-container links with atomic leaves (knowledge-v5-leaf-closure-round27-1).

statements.statementText: Simple manipulation of surds
notesText: Students should understand what surds represent and their use for exact answers. Manipulation will be very simple. For example:
statements.sourceLocator: Official specification PDF page 17, line IDs p17-r13-b0-l0,p17-r18-b0-l0,p17-r18-b0-l1,p17-r18-b0-l2,p17-r18-b1-l0,p17-r18-b1-l1,p17-r19-b0-l0,p17-r19-b0-l1,p17-r19-b0-l2,p17-r19-b1-l0, 1.C

reviewNotes: Directly matches the simplification and combination of surds.

statements.statementText: D Rationalising the denominator
statements.sourceLocator: Official specification PDF page 17, line IDs p17-r19-b2-l0,p17-r20-b0-l0,p17-r21-b0-l0,p17-r21-b0-l1,p17-r21-b0-l2,p17-r21-b0-l3,p17-r21-b0-l4,p17-r21-b0-l5, 1.D

reviewNotes: Directly matches rewriting fractions with surd denominators.

statements.statementText: The manipulation of quadratic expressions
notesText: Students should be able to factorise quadratic expressions and complete the square.
statements.sourceLocator: Official specification PDF page 18, line IDs p18-r5-b0-l0,p18-r6-b0-l0,p18-r6-b0-l1,p18-r6-b0-l2, OFFICIAL-Edexcel-4PM1-2.A

reviewNotes: Factorising is the primary manipulation
Completing the square is the other key manipulation

statements.statementText: The roots of a quadratic equation
notesText: Students should be able to use the discriminant to identify whether the roots are equal real, unequal real or not real.
statements.sourceLocator: Official specification PDF page 18, line IDs p18-r8-b0-l0,p18-r9-b0-l0,p18-r9-b0-l1,p18-r9-b0-l2,p18-r9-b0-l3, OFFICIAL-Edexcel-4PM1-2.B

conceptLinks.evidenceSpan: use the discriminant to identify whether the roots are equal real, unequal real or not real
reviewNotes: Discriminant directly determines nature of roots

statements.statementText: Simple examples involving functions of the roots of a quadratic equation
notesText: Students are expected to understand and use: ax² + bx + c = 0 has roots α, β = (−b ± √(b² − 4ac))/(2a), and forming an equation with given roots, which are expressed in terms of α and β: α + β = −b/a and αβ = c/a.

reviewNotes: Vieta's formulas linking roots and coefficients

conceptLinks.evidenceSpan: Simple examples involving functions of the roots
reviewNotes: Symmetric functions of roots

conceptLinks.evidenceSpan: ax² + bx + c = 0 has roots α, β = (−b ± √(b² − 4ac))/(2a)
reviewNotes: Students are expected to use the quadratic formula to find the roots, as explicitly stated in the note.

conceptLinks.evidenceSpan: forming an equation with given roots, which are expressed in terms of α and β
reviewNotes: Constructing a new quadratic equation whose roots are transformations of α and β, as stated in the note.

statements.statementText: Simple algebraic division
notesText: Division by (x + a), (x – a), (ax + b) or (ax – b) will be required.
statements.sourceLocator: Official specification PDF page 19, line IDs p19-r5-b0-l0,p19-r6-b0-l0,p19-r6-b0-l1, OFFICIAL-Edexcel-4PM1-3.A

conceptLinks.evidenceSpan: Division by (x + a), (x – a), (ax + b) or (ax – b)
reviewNotes: Polynomial division by linear expression

statements.statementText: The factor and remainder theorems
notesText: Students should know that if f(x) = 0 when x = a, then (x − a) is a factor of f(x). Students may be required to factorise cubic expressions such as: Students should be familiar with the terms ‘quotient’ and ‘remainder’ and be able to determine the remainder when the polynomial f(x) is divided by (ax + b) or (ax − b).
examplesText: x³ + 3x² − 4 and 6x³ + 11x² − x − 6, when a factor has been provided.
statements.sourceLocator: Official specification PDF page 19, line IDs p19-r10-b0-l0,p19-r11-b0-l0,p19-r11-b0-l1,p19-r12-b0-l0,p19-r13-b0-l0,p19-r14-b0-l0,p19-r14-b0-l1,p19-r14-b0-l2,p19-r14-b0-l3,p19-r14-b0-l4,p19-r8-b0-l0,p19-r9-b0-l0,p19-r9-b0-l1, OFFICIAL-Edexcel-4PM1-3.B

conceptLinks.evidenceSpan: if f(x) = 0 when x = a, then (x − a) is a factor
reviewNotes: Factor theorem
Application to cubic factorisation

conceptLinks.evidenceSpan: determine the remainder when the polynomial f(x) is divided by (ax + b) or (ax − b)
reviewNotes: Application of the remainder theorem: substituting x = -b/a yields the remainder.

statements.statementText: Solutions of equations, extended to include the simultaneous solution of one linear and one quadratic equation in two variables
notesText: The solution of a cubic equation containing at least one rational root may be set.
statements.sourceLocator: Official specification PDF page 19, line IDs p19-r15-b0-l0,p19-r15-b0-l1,p19-r15-b0-l2,p19-r17-b0-l0,p19-r17-b0-l1,p19-r17-b0-l2, OFFICIAL-Edexcel-4PM1-3.C

conceptLinks.evidenceSpan: simultaneous solution of one linear and one quadratic equation in two variables
reviewNotes: Directly matches

conceptLinks.evidenceSpan: The solution of a cubic equation containing at least one rational root may be set.
reviewNotes: Factor theorem used to find rational root
Cubic polynomial solving is an extension

statements.statementText: D Simple inequalities, linear and quadratic
examplesText: For example ax + b > cx + d, px² + qx + r < sx² + tx + u
statements.sourceLocator: Official specification PDF page 19, line IDs p19-r16-b0-l0,p19-r17-b1-l0,p19-r18-b0-l0, OFFICIAL-Edexcel-4PM1-3.D

reviewNotes: Example of linear inequality

conceptLinks.evidenceSpan: px² + qx + r < sx² + tx + u
reviewNotes: Example of quadratic inequality

statements.statementText: The graphical representation of linear inequalities in two variables
notesText: The emphasis will be on simple questions designed to test fundamental principles. Simple problems on linear programming may be set.
statements.sourceLocator: Official specification PDF page 19, line IDs p19-r19-b0-l0,p19-r19-b0-l1,p19-r20-b0-l0,p19-r20-b0-l1,p19-r20-b0-l2,p19-r20-b1-l0,p19-r20-b1-l1, OFFICIAL-Edexcel-4PM1-3.E

conceptLinks.evidenceSpan: graphical representation of linear inequalities in two variables
reviewNotes: Regions defined by inequalities

conceptLinks.evidenceSpan: Simple problems on linear programming may be set.
reviewNotes: Linear programming uses regions

statements.statementText: Graphs of polynomials and rational functions with linear denominators
notesText: The concept of asymptotes parallel to the coordinate axes is expected.
statements.sourceLocator: Official specification PDF page 20, line IDs p20-r5-b0-l0,p20-r5-b0-l1,p20-r6-b0-l0,p20-r6-b0-l1, OFFICIAL-Edexcel-4PM1-4.A

reviewNotes: Cubic graphs are a specific type of polynomial graph.
Quartic graphs are a specific type of polynomial graph.
Reciprocal functions of the form 1/(linear) are typical examples of rational functions with linear denominators.

conceptLinks.evidenceSpan: The concept of asymptotes parallel to the coordinate axes is expected.
reviewNotes: Asymptotes are a core concept for rational function graphs.

statements.statementText: The solution of equations and transcendental functions by graphical methods
notesText: Non-graphical iterative methods are not required.
statements.sourceLocator: Official specification PDF page 20, line IDs p20-r8-b0-l0,p20-r8-b0-l1,p20-r8-b0-l2,p20-r9-b0-l0,p20-r9-b0-l1, OFFICIAL-Edexcel-4PM1-4.B

conceptLinks.evidenceSpan: solution of equations and transcendental functions by graphical methods
reviewNotes: Graphical solution involves finding intersections of curves.

statements.statementText: Use of the ∑ notation
notesText: The ∑ notation may be employed wherever its use seems desirable.
statements.sourceLocator: Official specification PDF page 20, line IDs p20-r15-b0-l0,p20-r16-b0-l0,p20-r16-b0-l1, OFFICIAL-Edexcel-4PM1-5.A

reviewNotes: Codex final semantic review: The official statement explicitly requires sigma notation.

statements.statementText: Arithmetic and geometric series
notesText: Knowledge of the general term of an arithmetic series is required.
Use of the sum to n terms of an arithmetic series is required.
Knowledge of the general term of a geometric series is required.
Use of the sum to n terms of a finite geometric series is required.
Use of the sum to infinity of a convergent geometric series, including the use of |r| < 1 is required.
Proofs of the above are not required.
statements.sourceLocator: Official specification PDF page 20, line IDs p20-r18-b0-l0,p20-r19-b0-l0,p20-r19-b0-l1,p20-r19-b1-l0,p20-r19-b1-l1,p20-r19-b2-l0,p20-r19-b2-l1,p20-r19-b3-l0,p20-r19-b3-l1,p20-r19-b4-l0,p20-r19-b4-l1,p20-r19-b4-l2,p20-r19-b5-l0, OFFICIAL-Edexcel-4PM1-5.B

conceptLinks.evidenceSpan: general term of an arithmetic series
reviewNotes: Knowledge of general term

conceptLinks.evidenceSpan: general term of a geometric series
reviewNotes: Knowledge of general term

conceptLinks.evidenceSpan: sum to n terms of an arithmetic series
reviewNotes: Use of sum formula

conceptLinks.evidenceSpan: sum to n terms of a finite geometric series
reviewNotes: Use of sum formula

conceptLinks.evidenceSpan: sum to infinity of a convergent geometric series
reviewNotes: Infinite sum formula
Condition for convergence

statements.statementText: Use of the binomial series (1 + x)ⁿ
notesText: Use of the series when: (i) n is a positive integer; (ii) n is rational and |x| < 1. The validity condition for (ii) is expected.
statements.sourceLocator: Official specification PDF page 20, line IDs p20-r23-b1-l0,p20-r24-b0-l0,p20-r25-b0-l0,p20-r25-b1-l0,p20-r25-b2-l0,p20-r26-b0-l0,p20-r27-b0-l0,p20-r27-b0-l1, OFFICIAL-Edexcel-4PM1-6.A

reviewNotes: Expansion for positive integer power
General binomial series with validity condition

statements.statementText: The addition and subtraction of coplanar vectors and the multiplication of a vector by a scalar
notesText: Knowledge of the fact that if α₁a + β₁b = α₂a + β₂b, where a and b are non-parallel vectors, then α₁ = α₂ and β₁ = β₂, is expected.
statements.sourceLocator: Official specification PDF page 21, line IDs p21-r13-b0-l0,p21-r14-b0-l0,p21-r14-b0-l1,p21-r14-b0-l2,p21-r14-b0-l3,p21-r14-b0-l4,p21-r14-b0-l5,p21-r3-b0-l0,p21-r4-b0-l0,p21-r4-b0-l1,p21-r4-b0-l2, 7.A

conceptLinks.evidenceSpan: addition and subtraction of coplanar vectors
reviewNotes: Vector addition and subtraction are fundamental operations.

conceptLinks.evidenceSpan: multiplication of a vector by a scalar
reviewNotes: Scalar multiplication scales a vector's magnitude.

conceptLinks.evidenceSpan: where a and b are non-parallel vectors
reviewNotes: The uniqueness of representation requires a and b to be non-parallel, a condition related to parallel vectors.

statements.statementText: Components and resolved parts of a vector
notesText: Use of the vectors i and j will be expected.
statements.sourceLocator: Official specification PDF page 21, line IDs p21-r14-b1-l0,p21-r14-b1-l1,p21-r6-b0-l0, 7.B

reviewNotes: Resolving a vector into perpendicular components often uses trigonometry.

conceptLinks.evidenceSpan: Use of the vectors i and j will be expected.
reviewNotes: i and j are unit vectors, as included in VECT-BASIC-NOTN-UNIT.
The statement explicitly covers components of a vector, part of 2D vector terminology and notation.

statements.statementText: Magnitude of a vector
statements.sourceLocator: Official specification PDF page 21, line IDs p21-r8-b0-l0, 7.C

reviewNotes: Magnitude is the length of the vector.

statements.statementText: D Position vector
notesText: AB⃗ = OB⃗ − OA⃗ = b − a
statements.sourceLocator: Official specification PDF page 21, line IDs p21-r15-b0-l0,p21-r15-b0-l1,p21-r15-b0-l2,p21-r9-b0-l0, 7.D

reviewNotes: A position vector locates a point relative to the origin.
The equation expresses directed segment AB using position vectors, a direct application of expressing segments as vectors.

statements.statementText: Unit vector
statements.sourceLocator: Official specification PDF page 21, line IDs p21-r10-b0-l0, 7.E

reviewNotes: A unit vector has magnitude 1.

statements.statementText: Use of vectors to establish simple properties of geometrical figures
notesText: The ‘simple properties’ will, in general, involve collinearity, parallel lines and concurrency. Position vector of a point dividing the line AB in the ratio m : n is expected.
statements.sourceLocator: Official specification PDF page 21, line IDs p21-r12-b0-l0,p21-r12-b0-l1,p21-r15-b1-l0,p21-r15-b1-l1,p21-r15-b1-l2,p21-r15-b2-l0,p21-r15-b2-l1, 7.F

reviewNotes: Collinearity is one of the 'simple properties'.
Parallel lines are a simple property established with vectors.

conceptLinks.evidenceSpan: Position vector of a point dividing the line AB in the ratio m : n
reviewNotes: Ratio division is a key application of position vectors.
Concurrency is a geometric property that can be established using vectors, fitting the broad application of vectors to geometry.

statements.statementText: The distance between two points
notesText: The distance d between two points (x₁, y₁) and (x₂, y₂) is given by d² = (x₁ − x₂)² + (y₁ − y₂)².
statements.sourceLocator: Official specification PDF page 22, line IDs p22-r5-b0-l0,p22-r5-b0-l1,p22-r6-b0-l0,p22-r7-b0-l0, OFFICIAL-Edexcel-4PM1-8.A

reviewNotes: Uses the Pythagorean distance formula.

statements.statementText: The point dividing a line in a given ratio
notesText: The coordinates of the point dividing the line joining (x₁, y₁) and (x₂, y₂) in the ratio m : n are given by ((nx₁ + mx₂)/(m + n), (ny₁ + my₂)/(m + n)).
statements.sourceLocator: Official specification PDF page 22, line IDs p22-r10-b0-l0,p22-r10-b0-l1,p22-r10-b0-l2,p22-r11-b0-l0,p22-r11-b0-l1,p22-r11-b0-l2,p22-r11-b0-l3,p22-r11-b0-l4,p22-r11-b0-l5,p22-r9-b0-l0, OFFICIAL-Edexcel-4PM1-8.B

conceptLinks.evidenceSpan: The coordinates of the point dividing the line joining (x₁, y₁) and (x₂, y₂) in the ratio m : n are given by ((nx₁ + mx₂)/(m + n), (ny₁ + my₂)/(m + n)).
reviewNotes: Exact match for dividing a line segment in a given ratio.

statements.statementText: Gradient of a straight line joining two points
statements.sourceLocator: Official specification PDF page 22, line IDs p22-r12-b0-l0, OFFICIAL-Edexcel-4PM1-8.C

conceptLinks.evidenceSpan: Gradient of a straight line joining two points
reviewNotes: Gradient is change in y over change in x.

statements.statementText: D The straight line and its equation
notesText: The y = mx + c and y − y₁ = m(x − x₁) forms of the equation of a straight line are expected to be known.
The interpretation of ax + by = c as a straight line is expected to be known.
statements.sourceLocator: Official specification PDF page 22, line IDs p22-r13-b0-l0,p22-r14-b0-l0,p22-r14-b0-l1,p22-r14-b0-l2,p22-r14-b1-l0,p22-r14-b1-l1, OFFICIAL-Edexcel-4PM1-8.D

reviewNotes: The gradient-intercept form.
The point-gradient form.
Interpreting an equation as a straight line; equivalent to general form ax+by+c=0 after rearrangement.

statements.statementText: The condition for two lines to be parallel or to be perpendicular
statements.sourceLocator: Official specification PDF page 22, line IDs p22-r17-b0-l0,p22-r17-b0-l1, OFFICIAL-Edexcel-4PM1-8.E

reviewNotes: Codex final semantic review: The two coordinate-geometry conditions are explicit and independently assessable.
Codex final semantic review: The two coordinate-geometry conditions are explicit and independently assessable.

statements.statementText: Differentiation and integration of sums of multiples of powers of x (excluding integration of 1/x), sin ax, cos ax, eᵃˣ
notesText: No formal proofs of the results for axⁿ, sin ax, cos ax and eᵃˣ will be required.
statements.sourceLocator: Official specification PDF page 23, line IDs p23-r4-b0-l0,p23-r5-b0-l0,p23-r5-b0-l1,p23-r6-b0-l0,p23-r6-b0-l1,p23-r6-b1-l0,p23-r7-b0-l0,p23-r7-b0-l1,p23-r7-b0-l2, OFFICIAL-Edexcel-4PM1-9.A

conceptLinks.evidenceSpan: Differentiation and integration of sums of multiples of powers of x (excluding integration of 1/x), sin ax, cos ax, eᵃˣ
reviewNotes: Power rule for differentiation.

conceptLinks.evidenceSpan: Differentiation and integration of sums of multiples of powers of x (excluding integration of 1/x), sin ax, cos ax, eᵃˣ
reviewNotes: Differentiation of sin and cos.

conceptLinks.evidenceSpan: Differentiation and integration of sums of multiples of powers of x (excluding integration of 1/x), sin ax, cos ax, eᵃˣ
reviewNotes: Differentiation of exponential functions.

statements.statementText: Applications to simple linear kinematics and to determination of areas and volumes
notesText: Understanding how displacement, velocity and acceleration are related using calculus. The volumes will be obtained only by revolution about the coordinate axes.

Acceleration as derivative of velocity.
Velocity from integrating acceleration.
Displacement from integrating velocity.
Area under a curve using integration.
Volumes of revolution about axes.

statements.statementText: Maxima and minima
notesText: Maxima and minima problems may be set in the context of a practical problem. Justification of maxima and minima will be expected.

statements.statementText: Application of calculus to rates of change and connected rates of change
notesText: The emphasis will be on simple examples to test principles. A knowledge of d y ≈ d y d x d x for small dx is expected.

notesText: The formulae s = rθ and A = ½r²θ for a circle are expected to be known.

statements.statementText: B The three basic trigonometric ratios of angles of any magnitude (in degrees or radians) and their graphs
notesText: To include the exact values for sine, cosine and tangent of 30°, 45°, 60° (and the radian equivalents), and the use of these to find the trigonometric ratios of related values such as 120°, 300°

statements.statementText: Use of the sine and cosine formulae
notesText: General proofs of the sine and cosine formulae will not be required. The cosine formula will be given but other formulae are expected to be known. The area of a triangle in the form (1/2)ab sin C is expected to be known.

statements.statementText: G The use of the basic addition formulae of trigonometry
notesText: Formal proofs of sin(A ± B), cos(A ± B) formulae will not be required.
Questions using the formulae for sin(A ± B), cos(A ± B), tan(A ± B) may be set; the formulae will be on the formula sheet, for example: sin(A + B) = sin A cos B + cos A sin B.
Long questions, explicitly involving excessive manipulation, will not be set.
statements.sourceLocator: Official specification PDF page 24, line IDs p24-r23-b0-l0,p24-r23-b0-l1,p24-r26-b1-l0,p24-r26-b1-l1,p24-r26-b2-l0,p24-r27-b0-l0,p24-r27-b1-l0,p24-r27-b1-l1,p24-r28-b0-l0,p24-r29-b0-l0,p24-r29-b0-l1, 10.G

statements.statementText: H Solution of simple trigonometric equations for a given interval
notesText: Students should be able to solve equations such as:

reviewNotes: Statement directly mentions simple trigonometric equations.
Examples include equations with multiple angles.
Statement specifies solving within a given interval.
One example is a quadratic equation in sin after using identity.
Solving this example equation requires applying the Pythagorean identity sin²θ+cos²θ=1 to express all terms in one trigonometric function.
